How would I implement Pascal from scratch for a custom 24-bit ISA?  I have a working C compiler and Assembler (and emulator and FPGA hardware).  Was Pascal bootstapped in assembler or C and then written in Pascal?  Are there open source implementations with liberal licenses (BSD, MIT) that I can look at?  I do not want to port from a 16-bit or 32-bit system.  I prefer to write-from-scratch, clean-room implementation, possibly using already-written Pascal souce examples for testing my compiler.  I also want a debugger.  How should I start?  


Thought for 36s
Given your constraints, I would not start by trying to build a self-hosting Pascal compiler immediately.

You already have the right base:

working assembler

working C compiler

emulator + FPGA target

desire for a clean-room implementation

debugger requirement from day one

That points to a practical architecture:

Recommended architecture
Build Pascal in three stages:

Pascal front end in C

lexer

parser

AST

symbol tables

type checker

simple error recovery

Portable intermediate form

either a small stack VM / p-code

or a very small typed IR of your own

Back ends

first: interpreter for the IR/p-code on the host

second: native code generator for your 24-bit ISA

later: optional self-hosting compiler in Pascal

That is the lowest-risk path because it lets you validate semantics before you fight register allocation, calling conventions, and codegen bugs.

Historically, how Pascal was bootstrapped
The earliest Pascal compiler was not originally bootstrapped in assembler in the way many people assume. Niklaus Wirth’s first FORTRAN-based attempt was unsuccessful; the successful implementation was done in a C-like language called Scallop and then translated by hand into Pascal for bootstrapping. Many later Pascal compilers became self-hosting. A famous exception is GNU Pascal, which was written in C. Turbo Pascal, much later, was famously written in assembly for speed and size. 

So the historical pattern is roughly:

prototype in another language

get a working compiler

rewrite or re-express the compiler in Pascal

use the previous compiler to compile the new compiler

That is exactly the path I would recommend for your ISA as well.

What to target first: native code or p-code?
For your situation, I would start with a Pascal-S / Pascal-P style subset and a p-code-like intermediate machine.

Why:

Pascal semantics are nontrivial enough already: nested procedures, static scoping, sets, records, packed/unpacked data, file types, pass-by-reference, variant records, etc.

your ISA is 24-bit, which means you will be making many nonstandard representation decisions anyway

a p-code interpreter gives you a correctness oracle before native codegen

Historically, Pascal-P was created specifically as a compiler porting kit: Pascal source compiled to p-code for a virtual stack machine, then interpreted on each target. That was done to make Pascal portable. 

For a custom ISA, that strategy is still excellent.

What subset should you implement first
Do not begin with full ISO Pascal.

Begin with a clean educational subset:

Phase 0: expression language
integers only

constants

variables

assignment

arithmetic

comparisons

if, while

begin ... end

writeln-like builtin for debugging

Phase 1: real Pascal block structure
program

declarations

scalar types: integer, boolean, char

arrays with fixed bounds

records

procedures/functions

lexical nesting

local variables

pass-by-value and var parameters

Phase 2: features that force runtime design
strings or packed char arrays

sets

pointers

heap

files

case statements

standard procedures/functions

Phase 3: optional/full language
ISO-ish cleanup

conformance tests

self-hosting subset

For your first compiler, avoid:

packed types

files

variant records

conformant arrays

procedural parameters

goto

real numbers

Those features create a disproportionate amount of compiler/runtime complexity.

Clean-room strategy
Your instinct is good: use existing Pascal systems for ideas and tests, not for porting.

A good clean-room posture is:

read language reports and public docs

study high-level architecture of older Pascal compilers

do not copy implementation code

write your own grammar, AST, type system, runtime, and codegen

use existing Pascal example programs only as black-box test inputs

That gives you educational value and keeps the implementation genuinely yours.

Best implementation strategy for your toolchain
Since you already have C and assembler, I would do this:

Stage A — host-side compiler in C
Run on your current host machine.

Outputs either:

textual p-code / bytecode

or textual assembly for your 24-bit ISA

This lets you iterate quickly.

Stage B — target runtime
On your ISA:

tiny startup

memory map

stack/heap model

runtime support calls

optional p-code interpreter

Stage C — native backend
Replace or complement the p-code interpreter with native code generation.

Stage D — self-hosting subset
Once the compiler is stable enough, rewrite the front end in Pascal itself and compile it with your earlier compiler.

IR choice: stack machine vs register IR
For Pascal, a stack IR is the easiest starting point.

Why:

expressions map naturally

nested scopes can be handled without solving global register allocation early

procedure calls are simpler

p-code history gives you many good precedents

A tiny IR might look like:

LDC k load constant

LD level, offset

ST level, offset

ADD SUB MUL DIV

EQ NE LT LE GT GE

JMP label

JZ label

CALL proc_id

RET

ENTER locals_size

LEAVE

LDI STI for indirect access

IDX for array indexing

FIELD offset

Then later map those to your 24-bit ISA.

The hardest design decisions you need early
These are the places where Pascal compilers get real:

1. Lexical nesting and static links
Pascal’s nested procedures are one of the biggest differences from C.

You need a decision for nonlocal access:

static link per frame

or display

or closure-like environment objects

For a first implementation, use static links. They are simpler and faithful to classic Pascal.

Each frame should contain:

return address

dynamic link

static link

locals

temporaries / spills if needed

2. Data layout on a 24-bit ISA
Decide up front:

size of integer

size of pointer

alignment rules

representation of boolean

char width

array bound representation

set representation

If your ISA is naturally 24-bit, I would strongly consider:

integer = signed 24-bit

pointer = 24-bit

boolean = 24-bit 0/1

char = 8-bit, but stored with clear load/store conventions

align aggregates to 24-bit word boundaries unless you have compelling byte-density reasons

Packed representations can wait.

3. Calling convention
Define this before codegen:

who pushes params

who cleans stack

how function result is returned

where static link goes

which registers are caller/callee saved

how large structs/records are passed

For simplicity:

caller pushes arguments left-to-right or right-to-left, but pick one and freeze it

caller also pushes static link

callee builds frame

return value in a dedicated register if scalar, hidden pointer if aggregate

4. Runtime model
You need a tiny Pascal runtime for:

startup

bounds-check failure

nil deref failure

division by zero

heap alloc/free if pointers are supported

I/O stubs

exit / halt

Keep it tiny and callable from generated code.

Debugger: how to design it from the start
You said you also want a debugger. Good. Add debug support immediately, not later.

I would design the compiler around a symbol-rich listing/debug model.

Emit these artifacts for every compile
token dump

AST dump

symbol table dump

frame layout report

IR listing

native assembly listing

source line map

procedure metadata

Runtime debugger features
At minimum:

single-step by source line

single-step by IR instruction

breakpoints by source line

show call stack

show current procedure

show locals/parameters

inspect static chain

inspect arbitrary memory

inspect globals

inspect heap blocks

show array bounds and current indices

trap on runtime errors

Best UI split
You already have Rust/Yew/WASM skills, so I would split it this way:

compiler/debug metadata generator in C

emulator instrumentation hooks in Rust or current emulator code

browser debugger UI in Yew/WASM

Views worth having:

source pane

current AST / scope pane

call stack pane

frame layout pane

registers pane

memory pane

static-chain visualization

disassembly pane

IR pane

For Pascal specifically, one killer feature is a scope-aware variable inspector:
show locals, parameters, globals, and nonlocals via static-link traversal.

Recommended project plan
Milestone 1 — define the language subset
Write these documents first:

language.md

runtime.md

abi.md

ir.md

debug-format.md

test-plan.md

This matters more than code at the start.

Milestone 2 — host interpreter for Pascal AST
Before codegen, you can interpret the AST directly for a tiny subset.

Pros:

fastest way to validate parser + semantics

easiest way to debug language rules

Cons:

not reusable for target execution

Still, it is often worth doing for the first 2–3 milestones.

Milestone 3 — p-code generator + host p-code VM
This becomes your semantic reference implementation.

Milestone 4 — target p-code VM on your 24-bit ISA
Now real Pascal programs run on your emulator/FPGA without needing native codegen yet.

Milestone 5 — native code generator
Target your 24-bit ISA directly.

Milestone 6 — debugger integration
Hook source lines, symbols, frames, and memory inspection into the emulator UI.

Milestone 7 — self-hosting subset
Rewrite enough of the compiler in Pascal to compile itself.

Open-source implementations worth studying
You asked specifically about liberal licenses.

Good for ideas / architecture
Pascal-P / Pascal-P5 is historically important and directly relevant because it is a portable p-code Pascal family. The Pascal-P5 project is presented as the fifth version of the Zurich Pascal-P compiler and emphasizes ISO 7185 compliance. 

Free Pascal is real, mature, and valuable to study conceptually, but its compiler itself is not BSD/MIT. Its FAQ says applications produced with its RTL are allowed to be proprietary because the RTL uses a modified LGPL exception, but that does not make the compiler source permissively licensed. 

Stanford Pascal is interesting because it still uses p-code plus an interpreter, and the repo says the compiler files are Pascal/P-Code while the interpreter is in C. I did not find an explicit permissive license on the GitHub page I checked, so I would treat it as “study carefully, license unclear until verified.” 

Small permissive educational repos
These are useful for architecture ideas, not for production-grade reuse:

JayPascal — MIT, Pascal compiler in C#. 

XDPC — MIT-0, small educational Pascal compiler with native x86 codegen. Very relevant as a “small compiler that directly emits native code” example, even though it targets DOS/x86. 

Qwerasdzxc/Pascal-Compiler — Apache-2.0, modular educational compiler with interpreter and C code generator. Architecturally useful because it separates lexer/parser/symbolizer/generator/runner. 

One caution: many permissively licensed Pascal repos on GitHub are student or educational compilers, not robust reference implementations. They are good for structure, testing ideas, and scope control, but not for blindly trusting semantics.

What I would personally avoid
For your project, I would avoid:

porting Turbo Pascal ideas too literally

implementing full ISO Pascal before subset stability

designing codegen before you define frame layout and static-scope access

adding real numbers early

building an optimizing compiler first

trying to self-host too soon

Suggested first deliverable
Your first usable milestone should be:

“Compile a small Pascal subset on the host into p-code, run it in a p-code VM on the emulator, and debug it with source-line stepping.”

That gives you:

proof of parsing

proof of semantic analysis

proof of runtime model

proof of debugger architecture

proof of target execution

before native code generation complexity arrives.

A concrete starting subset
I would start with exactly this grammar surface:

program

const

var

scalar integer/boolean

fixed arrays

procedures with parameters

function returning integer

begin/end

assignment

if/then/else

while/do

relational operators

arithmetic operators

writeln builtin

one-dimensional arrays only

That is enough to validate:

scopes

frames

calls

control flow

memory layout

debugger variable display

My recommendation in one sentence
Start with a clean-room Pascal-S/Pascal-P style subset in C, targeting a small p-code VM first, build the debugger metadata and frame model from day one, and only then add a native 24-bit code generator and eventual self-hosting.

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete architecture.md + milestone plan + test matrix + debugger metadata format tailored to your 24-bit ISA.


So I want to split this project into layers.  A low-level p-code interpreter and possibly run-time.  How is memory typically managed in Pascal, malloc/free or gc or other?  Pascal would be the higher-level tooling to compile to the p-code.  Other languaged that compile to p-code would follow.


Thought for 8s
You want the project split into layers, with a low-level p-code machine/runtime first, and then Pascal as one front end targeting that machine, with room for other languages later. That is a good direction.

It gives you:

a stable execution substrate,

a semantic reference model before native codegen,

a reusable toolchain for multiple languages.

The key design question now is the runtime model: stack, heap, globals, code, metadata, and how dynamic memory works.

Short answer on Pascal memory management
Classic Pascal was not generally garbage-collected. The usual model was:

stack allocation for locals, parameters, temporaries

static/global storage for globals

heap allocation for dynamically created structures via new

explicit reclamation, usually via dispose, or sometimes effectively “free everything at program end”

That is still the cleanest model for your first implementation. Standard Pascal and mainstream descendants historically center on explicit heap management rather than GC. Free Pascal’s docs and wiki still document New/Dispose and GetMem/FreeMem style management, and its heap debugging support tracks allocation/deallocation rather than automatic tracing GC. 

So for your first p-code runtime, I would recommend:

no GC initially

explicit heap operations

optional region/arena reset at process end

later, optional reference counting or tracing GC as a runtime extension for other languages

Best layering for your project
I would split the system like this:

Layer 0 — target substrate
Your existing pieces:

assembler

emulator

FPGA hardware

low-level monitor/debug hooks

native calling convention for your 24-bit ISA

This layer should know nothing about Pascal.

Layer 1 — p-code VM / abstract machine
This becomes the real foundation.

It should define:

instruction set

value representation

activation record/frame layout

globals model

heap model

runtime traps

debug metadata hooks

Think of this as your language-neutral machine.

Historically, Pascal p-code systems such as UCSD Pascal used a stack machine model, with a shared stack for expression evaluation and procedure frames. That is exactly the right family of design here. 

Layer 2 — base runtime services
This is not “Pascal runtime” yet. It is the minimal substrate any front end can rely on:

heap allocator

string/array block helpers

bounds-check failure handler

nil-pointer failure handler

divide-by-zero handler

assertion/trap support

I/O hooks

debug symbol and source mapping support

Layer 3 — Pascal-specific runtime
Only put Pascal-specific semantics here:

new, dispose

read, write, writeln

Pascal file model, if you add it later

set operations, if not in the VM

conformant-array helpers, if you ever add them

Pascal string helpers, if you extend beyond packed char arrays

Layer 4 — front ends
Start with:

Pascal compiler → p-code

Later:

tiny BASIC → p-code

structured Forth-like front end → p-code

tiny Lisp → p-code

maybe a C subset → p-code

That is a strong architecture because the VM becomes your shared semantic platform.

How memory is usually organized in a Pascal system
A practical memory map for your p-code machine would be:

+--------------------+ high memory
| debugger scratch   |
| VM bookkeeping     |
+--------------------+
| heap               |  grows upward
|                    |
+--------------------+
| free space         |
+--------------------+
| stack              |  grows downward
| call frames        |
| eval stack         |
+--------------------+
| globals/statics    |
+--------------------+
| constants/literals |
+--------------------+
| p-code / bytecode  |
+--------------------+ low memory
That is a very natural fit for an emulator and FPGA target.

Separate or unified stack?
You have two choices:

Option A — one unified stack
Used for:

procedure frames

expression temporaries

This matches classic p-code machine design well. UCSD p-Machine is described as using one shared stack for both procedure stack frames and instruction operands. 

Pros

simple VM

fewer pointers/registers

historically faithful

Cons

debugger is slightly harder

expression stack corruption can damage frames if your VM is buggy

less convenient for multiple languages with different evaluation models

Option B — call stack + eval stack
Separate:

call stack for frames

eval stack for p-code operands

Pros

cleaner debugger

better invariants

easier to support multiple front ends later

easier to reason about stack depth separately from frame layout

Cons

slightly more VM state

a bit less “classic”

For your project, I would choose separate stacks unless you specifically want historical UCSD-like fidelity. It makes debugging much easier.

Recommended runtime memory model
For a first clean-room system, I would use four storage classes.

1. Globals/statics
Allocated once when program loads.

Use for:

global variables

typed constants that need storage

runtime state

2. Stack
Used for:

parameters

locals

temporaries

return address / dynamic link / static link

This is where Pascal naturally lives for ordinary code.

3. Heap
Used for:

pointers created by new

dynamic records

dynamic nodes for trees/lists

later, strings or managed arrays if you choose

4. Code/metadata space
Used for:

bytecode / p-code

literal pools

source line tables

symbol tables for debugger

What should heap allocation look like?
For Pascal first version: explicit allocation
I recommend these VM/runtime operations:

HEAP_ALLOC size -> ptr

HEAP_FREE ptr

HEAP_ZERO ptr, size

optional HEAP_REALLOC only if another language later wants it

Pascal front end then maps:

new(p) → allocate size of pointed-to type, assign pointer

dispose(p) → free and optionally nil the pointer

maybe mark/release as a nonstandard extension later

That gives you a small, understandable runtime.

Should the heap be malloc/free style?
Yes, for the first version.

Even if the source language says new/dispose, under the hood that is basically a typed wrapper around an allocator API equivalent to malloc/free. Free Pascal’s documentation explicitly distinguishes Pascal-level New/Dispose from lower-level GetMem/FreeMem, which supports this layered interpretation. 

So the layering can be:

VM allocator: raw blocks

Pascal runtime: typed heap ops on top

That is clean.

Should Pascal use GC?
Not initially.

Reasons:

not historically typical for classic Pascal

adds runtime and debugger complexity

muddies semantics while you are still validating the language

not needed to get real Pascal programs working

You can add GC later if another language benefits from it more.

Good heap strategies for your VM
For your hardware and emulator, here are the realistic options.

Option 1 — bump allocator only
Allocate by moving a heap pointer upward.

No free except “reset all”.

Pros

tiny

perfect for bring-up

very easy to debug

Cons

cannot support true dispose

leaks until program end

Good for:

compiler bring-up

tests

short-running tools

Option 2 — free-list allocator
Maintain block headers and a free list.

Pros

simple enough to implement

supports dispose

realistic for Pascal

Cons

fragmentation

coalescing logic

debugger must understand headers

This is the best first “real” allocator.

Option 3 — segregated size classes / slab style
Useful later for managed runtimes or object-heavy languages.

Pros

fast

predictable

Cons

more complexity

overkill initially

Option 4 — tracing GC
Mark-sweep, copying, or generational.

Pros

future-proof for Lisp-like languages

fewer leaks in managed languages

Cons

root scanning complexity

interaction with stack maps

debugger complexity

moving GC complicates raw pointer semantics

Not for version 1.

My recommendation for your first allocator
Implement two modes:

Bring-up mode
bump allocator

no free

reset heap between program runs

Real mode
free-list allocator with headers

block size

free/used bit

maybe next/prev free links

optional coalescing on free

This gives you:

fastest path to first success

smooth transition to a usable Pascal runtime

Pascal-specific semantic model for memory
Pascal usually feels cleaner than C because most ordinary objects are not heap allocated.

Typical pattern:

local records/arrays: stack

globals: static

only pointer-based structures: heap

That means your front end should aggressively prefer stack/static placement and use heap only when the source says so.

Suggested mapping
Variables
local scalar/record/array → frame offset

global scalar/record/array → global segment offset

Parameters
by value → copied into frame or parameter area

var parameter → address passed in

Functions/procedures
frame contains locals and bookkeeping

static link for nested scopes

Pointers
24-bit pointer to heap object or global/static object

nil = 0 is simplest, unless address 0 is valid in your architecture

Suggested p-code machine operations for memory
I would define VM instructions along these lines.

Addressing/storage
LDG offset — load global

STG offset — store global

LDL offset — load local

STL offset — store local

LDA_LOCAL offset — push address of local

LDA_GLOBAL offset — push address of global

LOAD_IND size

STORE_IND size

Frame/nonlocal access
FOLLOW_STATIC depth

LDN depth, offset

STN depth, offset

LDA_NONLOCAL depth, offset

Heap
ALLOC size

FREE

MEMZERO size

MEMCOPY size

Checking
CHK_BOUNDS lo, hi

CHK_NIL

CHK_TAG if you later add tagged objects

TRAP code

Aggregate helpers
INDEX elem_size, low_bound

FIELD offset

This instruction set can support Pascal cleanly and still be reusable for other languages.

Should the runtime be Pascal-specific or generic?
You said other languages may follow. So keep the lower runtime generic.

Generic VM/runtime should own
raw allocation

raw memory movement

stack/frame model

trap handling

debug hooks

primitive I/O abstraction

Pascal front end/runtime should own
static typing rules

new / dispose

var parameter semantics

nested procedure semantics

Pascal built-ins

Pascal standard type semantics

That split lets you later add languages with different memory models.

For example:

Pascal → explicit allocation

Lisp → GC heap

BASIC → dynamic strings + simple reference counting

Forth-like language → manual dictionary arena

Same VM, different front-end/runtime layers.

If other languages will follow, what memory model should the VM expose?
A good neutral model is:

VM object kinds
Not source-language-visible, just runtime categories:

raw block

string block

array block

record/object block

closure/env block

runtime descriptor block

You do not need this all up front, but it is helpful to reserve the concept.

Minimal neutral API
At the VM/runtime boundary:

alloc raw block

free raw block

read/write primitive

copy/fill

query size

optional type tag in header

optional finalizer hook later

That is enough to host both manual-memory and managed-memory languages.

Debugger implications
If memory matters, design the allocator and frames so the debugger can introspect them.

Heap block header suggestion
Each allocated block could have:

struct HeapHeader {
    u24 size_words;
    u24 flags;
    u24 type_id;      // optional
    u24 prev_or_next; // depending on allocator design
}
Useful flags:

allocated/free

Pascal object

array

string

record

internal runtime block

maybe “poisoned/freed”

Then your debugger can show:

live heap blocks

free blocks

leaks on exit

block ownership/type

double free attempts

use-after-free if you poison blocks in debug mode

Frame record suggestion
Each call frame should expose:

return PC

previous frame pointer / dynamic link

static link

procedure id

locals base

eval stack base snapshot

That makes nested Pascal debugging much easier.

A practical staged plan
Phase 1 — define the VM first
Write documents for:

vm-isa.md

vm-memory.md

vm-abi.md

vm-debug.md

Do this before writing much code.

Phase 2 — build a tiny VM with no Pascal yet
Support:

constants

arithmetic

branches

calls

locals/globals

heap alloc/free

traps

line stepping

Then hand-write small p-code tests.

Phase 3 — add Pascal subset front end
Compile a tiny subset to p-code:

integers

booleans

assignment

if

while

procedures

arrays

records

new / dispose

Phase 4 — add debugger
Debugger should show:

p-code instruction stream

current line

frame chain

static chain

locals

globals

heap blocks

Phase 5 — let a second language target the same VM
This will force you to keep the VM generic rather than accidentally baking Pascal into it.

Concrete recommendation on memory policy
If I were designing this for your 24-bit platform, I would do this:

VM v1
separate call stack and eval stack

globals segment

heap with bump allocator in bring-up mode

free-list allocator in normal mode

no GC

explicit traps

pointer is 24-bit

debug headers on every heap block in debug builds

Pascal v1
no GC

new / dispose

stack for locals

globals for statics

heap only for pointers

optional leak report at process exit

runtime bounds checks enabled by default

Later multi-language extension
Add optional alternate heap managers:

manual heap

reference-counted heap

tracing GC heap

and let each front end choose one.

That avoids forcing Pascal into a GC design it does not need, while still leaving room for Lisp-like or scripting languages later.

Bottom line
For your layered architecture, I would treat Pascal as a typed front end over a generic p-code VM, and I would use explicit heap management, not GC, for the first runtime.

That means:

stack for locals and temporaries

globals for static storage

heap for pointer-based dynamic objects

new / dispose at the Pascal level

allocator API underneath that is basically malloc/free in spirit

GC only later, as an optional runtime mode for other languages

That is historically aligned with Pascal, technically simpler, and a better base for a debugger and for additional languages. 

I can turn this into a concrete vm-memory.md and pcode-opcodes.md draft next.


Okay, in order to write tests, I'd like to introduce a p-code-assembler. Does tha make sense?  It would be a subset of Pascal not sharing Pascal idioms, but enough to create Hello World, toggle LEDs, read/write UART.   It would have a simple calling convention, not support typing per-sed, or complex data structures.  The target ISA is integer only, so no floating point.  Does this make sense?

Yes — that makes a lot of sense.

In fact, for your project I think a p-code assembler is one of the best early moves, because it lets you separate three problems that otherwise get tangled together:

VM design

runtime / calling convention design

Pascal front-end correctness

If you skip the assembler, every early failure is ambiguous:

is the VM wrong?

is the runtime wrong?

is the code generator wrong?

is the parser wrong?

is the Pascal semantic analysis wrong?

A p-code assembler gives you a way to test the VM and runtime directly, with tiny hand-written programs.

Restating the architecture
You are proposing a layered system like this:

Layer A — p-code VM
Executes a small instruction set.

Layer B — p-code assembler
Human-writable source language for tests, demos, low-level runtime bring-up.

Layer C — runtime library / monitor bindings
UART, LED, memory, traps, maybe stack/heap helpers.

Layer D — Pascal compiler
Compiles Pascal into p-code.

Layer E — later front ends
Other languages also target the same p-code.

That is a very good architecture.

The p-code assembler becomes for your VM what assembly is for a real ISA:

a precise test tool

a bootstrap tool

a debugger input language

a way to write tiny runtime pieces before Pascal is ready

Why this is a strong idea
1. It gives you direct VM tests
You will want tests like:

push constants, add, print result

branch on zero

call/return

access locals/globals

follow static/dynamic links if you have them

allocate/free heap block

write UART

toggle LED

bounds trap

nil trap

These are much easier to express in a tiny assembly-like language than in Pascal.

2. It lets you bootstrap the runtime before Pascal exists
Before the Pascal compiler works, you still need:

startup

I/O shims

debug/trap handlers

maybe memory helpers

A p-code assembler lets you write these as VM-level programs or modules.

3. It clarifies the calling convention early
You said “simple calling convention.” Good.

The assembler forces you to define:

what a call frame looks like

argument passing

return values

local storage reservation

stack cleanup rules

That discipline helps both the VM and Pascal backend.

4. It gives you reproducible debugger cases
Debugger bring-up is hard if every test comes from a buggy compiler.

With p-asm, you can hand-write:

a three-function call chain

a recursive countdown

a loop with locals

a UART output routine

a deliberate trap

Then verify stepping, breakpoints, stack display, and memory inspection.

My recommendation: make it explicitly not Pascal
I agree with your instinct that it should not share Pascal idioms.

Do not try to make it “tiny Pascal.”

Make it look like a machine-oriented textual form.

That keeps the layers clean:

Pascal is a source language

p-asm is a target-language authoring tool

That distinction is healthy.

What the p-code assembler should be
Think of it as:

untyped or lightly typed

integer-only

explicit control flow

explicit loads/stores

explicit call frames

explicit memory operations

labels

directives for globals, constants, strings, imports/exports

It should feel closer to:

bytecode assembly

threaded-code assembly

simple VM macro assembly

than to Pascal or C.

Good scope for v1
You want enough to write:

Hello World

LED toggle

UART read/write

simple loops

simple function calls

stack and global tests

That suggests a very small feature set.

Source language features
labels

procedures/functions

constants

string literals

integer literals

comments

directives for globals/data

instructions

optional macros later

No need initially for
type declarations

structs

arrays in source syntax

records

source-level pointers

floating point

polymorphism

expression parsing beyond maybe trivial immediate syntax

A good design principle
The assembler should expose the VM’s semantics almost 1:1.

So if the VM has an instruction like:

PUSH 42

LOADG uart_status

JZ loop

CALL putc

RET

then the assembler should map directly to that.

Avoid “convenient” high-level sugar too early.

You want this tool mainly for:

clarity

testing

determinism

not productivity.

One key decision: stack machine vs explicit locals model
Since this is p-code, the natural design is a stack-oriented assembler.

Example style:

proc main
    push 72
    call putc
    push 105
    call putc
    push 10
    call putc
    halt
endproc
But for debugging and maintainability, I recommend you also support named local slots or explicit frame offsets.

For example:

proc blink locals 1
loop:
    push 1
    out led
    call delay
    push 0
    out led
    call delay
    jmp loop
endproc
or, more machine-like:

proc blink 1
loop:
    push 1
    storeg LED_PORT
    call delay
    push 0
    storeg LED_PORT
    call delay
    jmp loop
endproc
That is enough.

Suggested minimum p-code VM model
For your custom integer-only machine, I would keep the VM itself integer-only too.

Core VM state
pc — p-code instruction pointer

fp — frame pointer

sp — eval stack pointer

gp — globals base, or implicit globals segment

maybe hp — heap pointer if heap exists now

Core data model
one scalar word type: signed/unsigned 24-bit integer value

addresses are also word-sized

byte loads/stores handled explicitly if needed for UART and strings

That is enough for quite a while.

Suggested instruction categories
Stack / constants
push <imm>

dup

drop

swap

over

Arithmetic / logic
add

sub

mul

div

mod

neg

and

or

xor

not

Comparison
eq

ne

lt

le

gt

ge

These can push 0/1.

Control flow
jmp <label>

jz <label>

jnz <label>

call <proc>

ret

halt

trap <code>

Frame/local/global access
enter <nlocals>

leave

loadl <offset>

storel <offset>

loadg <symbol>

storeg <symbol>

addrg <symbol>

addrl <offset>

Indirect memory
load

store

loadb

storeb

Optional runtime
alloc <size>

free

sys <id> or hostcall <id>

You may not need heap yet, but having the slot reserved is useful.

Calling convention recommendation
Since this is for bring-up and testing, make the convention brutally simple.

Suggested convention
arguments are pushed on the eval stack

call foo

callee uses enter nlocals

arguments become addressable relative to frame pointer

return value is pushed on the eval stack before ret

caller removes args if needed, or callee does — pick one and stick to it

For simplicity, I would choose:

Better v1 convention
caller pushes args

call foo

callee reads args at known negative offsets from fp

callee leaves one return value on the eval stack, or none for procedures

callee pops its frame only

arguments are cleaned by callee or encoded in ret n

A nice compact form is:

ret 0 for no args

ret 2 meaning pop 2 args and return

or retv 2 meaning return value + pop 2 args

But that may complicate the VM slightly.

A simpler alternative:

keep call frames separate from eval stack

copy args into frame slots on call

That is cleaner but a bit more machinery.

For early work, either is fine. If you expect lots of debugging, a separate frame area is easier to reason about.

For your use case, I would recommend two abstractions for I/O
Since you want UART and LED access, decide whether p-code talks to:

Option A — VM-level abstract devices
Example:

sys PUTC

sys GETC

sys LED_WRITE

Pros

portable across host interpreter and real hardware

easy to test

easier debugger integration

Cons

less “metal”

Option B — memory-mapped addresses
Example:

push UART_DATA

push 65

storeb

Pros

closer to the hardware

fewer abstractions

Cons

less portable

awkward for hosted tests

slightly harder to evolve

Best answer: support both
Use memory-mapped addresses in the runtime if you want, but also provide small VM/syscall abstractions for common tasks.

For example:

putc implemented as runtime p-asm

inside it, either call sys PUTC on host, or do memory-mapped UART on target

That keeps the assembler small and the runtime portable.

Hello World / LED / UART are perfect early test cases
These are exactly the right kinds of tests.

Hello World tests
Validates:

string/data section handling

loops

loadb/storeb

call/return

UART output

LED toggle tests
Validates:

MMIO writes

loops

delay call structure

integration with emulator/FPGA

UART read/write
Validates:

polling loops

conditional branching

MMIO reads and writes

byte operations

Those will shake out most VM bugs quickly.

A concrete source-language sketch
Something like this would work well:

.const UART_DATA   0xFF0100
.const UART_STATUS 0xFF0101
.const LED_PORT    0xFF0200

.proc putc
    ; arg0 at fp-1
wait:
    push UART_STATUS
    loadb
    push 1
    and
    jz wait

    push UART_DATA
    loadarg 0
    storeb
    ret 1
.end

.proc puts
loop:
    loadarg 0
    loadb
    dup
    jz done
    call putc
    loadarg 0
    push 1
    add
    storearg 0
    jmp loop
done:
    drop
    ret 1
.end

.data msg "Hello, world!\n", 0

.proc main
    push msg
    call puts
    halt
.end
I am not saying this exact syntax is best, only that this level of abstraction is appropriate.

One subtle point: typed vs untyped
You said “not support typing per se.”

That is right for the assembler source language. But the VM still needs operational widths.

Even in an “untyped” p-code assembler, you will still need distinctions like:

word load/store

byte load/store

signed vs unsigned compare, maybe

address vs integer conventions, even if both are same-sized

So the source language can be untyped, but the instruction set should still be explicit enough to avoid ambiguity.

For example:

load = word

loadb = byte

lt = signed compare

ltu = unsigned compare if needed later

This will matter for UART, chars, string traversal, and array/string runtime work.

Should the assembler include macros?
Not initially.

For v1, I would keep it extremely small:

labels

constants

procedures

data/string directives

instructions

Then after the VM is stable, add:

.macro

.include

conditional assembly

symbolic frame slot aliases

Macros are helpful later, but they can hide VM design problems early.

What to test first
I would define a staged test suite.

Stage 1 — pure VM instruction tests
push/add/sub

dup/drop/swap

compare/branch

simple halt

Stage 2 — procedure tests
one call

nested calls

recursion

return value

locals

Stage 3 — memory tests
globals

indirect load/store

byte load/store

string walk

Stage 4 — device tests
UART output

UART polling input

LED on/off

blink loop

Stage 5 — error tests
bad opcode

stack underflow

invalid memory access

explicit trap

These tests should exist before Pascal codegen starts producing real programs.

This also helps your future debugger design
A p-code assembler pairs naturally with a debugger.

You can support debug info at two levels:

Level 1 — instruction/label level
current pc

current proc

label names

stack contents

frame contents

Level 2 — source mapping
p-asm source line to p-code address

later Pascal source line to p-code address

That means your debugger can be useful before Pascal exists.

This is a big win.

One thing I would avoid
I would avoid making the p-code assembler too much like a “portable assembly language for the target ISA.”

It should target the VM, not become a second real ISA project.

Otherwise you risk re-creating a complicated lower-level compiler target and losing the simplicity benefit.

So keep it:

stack-based

compact

procedural

explicit

test-friendly

My recommendation
Yes, introduce a p-code assembler now.

Make it:

machine-like, not Pascal-like

integer-only

stack-oriented

simple calling convention

labels + directives + instructions

enough for:

Hello World

LED toggle

UART I/O

loops

calls

globals

byte/word memory access

And use it for:

VM bring-up

runtime bring-up

debugger bring-up

regression tests

hand-authored demos

That is a very strong foundation for Pascal and later languages.

Recommended next concrete artifacts
Write these first:

pvm.md
VM state

memory layout

stack model

traps

pasm.md
syntax

directives

labels

procedures

calling convention

opcode-table.md
every opcode

operands

stack effect

semantics

trap conditions

tests/
hand-written .pasm test programs

expected stdout / memory / trap results

The best next step is probably to define the actual pasm syntax and the first 20–30 opcodes.

   124→## 3. Call Frame Layout
   125→
   126→When `call` executes, a new frame is pushed onto the call stack:
   127→
   128→```
   129→         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
   130→         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
   131→         │ ...              │
   132→         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
   133→fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
   134→         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
   135→         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (lexically enclosing frame)
   136→         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
   137→         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
   138→         ├──────────────────┤
   139→         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
   140→         │ ...              │
   141→         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
   142→         └──────────────────┘
   143→```
   144→
   145→- **Caller** pushes arguments onto eval stack, then `call addr`
   146→- **call** instruction: pops args from eval stack into call stack frame header,
   147→  pushes return PC, dynamic link, static link, proc ID
   148→- **enter N**: advances csp by N*3 to reserve local slots, sets fp
   149→- **leave**: restores fp to dynamic link, restores csp
   150→- **ret N**: pushes return value (if any) to eval stack, pops frame, cleans N args
   151→
   152→## 4. Calling Convention
   153→
   154→Simple and explicit:
   155→
   156→1. Caller pushes arguments left-to-right onto eval stack
   157→2. Caller executes `call <proc_addr>`
   158→3. Callee executes `enter <nlocals>` (reserves local slots)
   159→4. Callee accesses args via `loada 0`, `loada 1`, etc.
   160→5. Callee accesses locals via `loadl 0`, `loadl 1`, etc.
   161→6. For functions: callee pushes return value onto eval stack
   162→7. Callee executes `ret <nargs>` (cleans args from frame)
   163→8. Caller finds return value (if any) on eval stack

   168→
   169→- `.spc` — p-code assembler source files (input to pasm)
   170→- `.p24` — assembled p-code bytecode files (output of pasm, input to VM)

   177→.const UART_DATA  -65280      ; named constant

   179→.const LED_PORT   -65024

   181→.global counter 1             ; 1 word of global storage
   182→.global buffer 32             ; 32 words
   183→
   184→.data msg 72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 0   ; "Hello\0" as bytes
   185→
   186→.proc main 0                 ; procedure, 0 locals
   187→    push msg                  ; push address of msg
   188→    call puts
   189→    halt
   190→.end
   191→
   192→.proc puts 1                 ; 1 local (loop pointer)
   193→    loada 0                   ; arg0 = string address
   194→    storel 0                  ; local0 = current ptr
   195→loop:
   196→    loadl 0
   197→    loadb                     ; load byte at ptr
   198→    dup
   199→    jz done                   ; if zero, done
   200→    sys 1                     ; PUTC
   201→    loadl 0

   203→    add

   206→done:

# pv24a — Design Document

## 1. VM State

The p-code VM maintains the following state in a memory struct:

```
vm_state:
    .word 0     ; pc   — p-code instruction pointer
    .word 0     ; esp  — eval stack pointer (top of eval stack)
    .word 0     ; csp  — call stack pointer (top of call stack)
    .word 0     ; fp   — current frame pointer (call stack)
    .word 0     ; gp   — globals base address
    .word 0     ; hp   — heap pointer (next free)
    .word 0     ; code — base address of p-code segment
    .word 0     ; status — 0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped
    .word 0     ; trap_code — last trap reason
```

## 2. P-Code Instruction Set

### 2.1 Stack / Constants

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `push` imm24 | 4 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push 24-bit immediate |
| `push_s` imm8 | 2 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push sign-extended 8-bit immediate |
| `dup` | 1 byte | ( a -- a a ) | Duplicate top |
| `drop` | 1 byte | ( a -- ) | Discard top |
| `swap` | 1 byte | ( a b -- b a ) | Swap top two |
| `over` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a b a ) | Copy second to top |

### 2.2 Arithmetic / Logic

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `add` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a+b ) | Add |
| `sub` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a-b ) | Subtract |
| `mul` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a*b ) | Multiply |
| `div` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a/b ) | Divide (trap on b=0) |
| `mod` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a%b ) | Modulo (trap on b=0) |
| `neg` | 1 byte | ( a -- -a ) | Negate |
| `and` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a&b ) | Bitwise AND |
| `or` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a\|b ) | Bitwise OR |
| `xor` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a^b ) | Bitwise XOR |
| `not` | 1 byte | ( a -- ~a ) | Bitwise NOT |
| `shl` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a<<n ) | Shift left |
| `shr` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a>>n ) | Arithmetic shift right |

### 2.3 Comparison

All comparisons push 1 (true) or 0 (false):

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `eq` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Equal |
| `ne` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Not equal |
| `lt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less than (signed) |
| `le` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less or equal (signed) |
| `gt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater than (signed) |
| `ge` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater or equal (signed) |

### 2.4 Control Flow

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `jmp` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( -- ) | Unconditional jump |
| `jz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if zero |
| `jnz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if nonzero |
| `call` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call procedure (static link = 0) |
| `calln` depth8 addr24 | 5 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call with static link (depth=0: nested, depth=N: N levels up) |
| `ret` nargs8 | 2 bytes | ( [retval] -- [retval] ) | Return, clean nargs |
| `halt` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Stop VM |
| `trap` code8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Trigger trap |

### 2.5 Local / Global / Nonlocal Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `enter` nlocals8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Set up frame, reserve N local slots |
| `leave` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Tear down frame |
| `loadl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load local at offset |
| `storel` off8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store local at offset |
| `loadg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load global at offset |
| `storeg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store global at offset |
| `addrl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of local |
| `addrg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of global |
| `loadn` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load nonlocal via static link chain |
| `storen` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store nonlocal via static link chain |

### 2.6 Indirect Memory Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `load` | 1 byte | ( addr -- val ) | Load word from address |
| `store` | 1 byte | ( val addr -- ) | Store word to address |
| `loadb` | 1 byte | ( addr -- byte ) | Load byte (zero-extended) |
| `storeb` | 1 byte | ( byte addr -- ) | Store byte |

### 2.7 System / I/O

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `sys` id8 | 2 bytes | (varies) | System call |

System call IDs:

| ID | Name | Stack Effect | Description |
|----|------|-------------|-------------|
| 0 | HALT | ( -- ) | Stop execution |
| 1 | PUTC | ( char -- ) | Write byte to UART |
| 2 | GETC | ( -- char ) | Read byte from UART (blocking) |
| 3 | LED | ( state -- ) | Write LED state |
| 4 | ALLOC | ( size -- ptr ) | Bump-allocate heap block |
| 5 | FREE | ( ptr -- ) | Free heap block (no-op in bump mode) |

### 2.8 Argument Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `loada` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load argument by index |
| `storea` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store argument by index |

## 3. Call Frame Layout

When `call` executes, a new frame is pushed onto the call stack:

```
         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
         │ ...              │
         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (lexically enclosing frame)
         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
         ├──────────────────┤
         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
         │ ...              │
         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
         └──────────────────┘
```

- **Caller** pushes arguments onto eval stack, then `call addr`
- **call** instruction: pops args from eval stack into call stack frame header,
  pushes return PC, dynamic link, static link, proc ID
- **enter N**: advances csp by N*3 to reserve local slots, sets fp
- **leave**: restores fp to dynamic link, restores csp
- **ret N**: pushes return value (if any) to eval stack, pops frame, cleans N args

## 4. Calling Convention

Simple and explicit:

1. Caller pushes arguments left-to-right onto eval stack
2. Caller executes `call <proc_addr>`
3. Callee executes `enter <nlocals>` (reserves local slots)
4. Callee accesses args via `loada 0`, `loada 1`, etc.
5. Callee accesses locals via `loadl 0`, `loadl 1`, etc.
6. For functions: callee pushes return value onto eval stack
7. Callee executes `ret <nargs>` (cleans args from frame)
8. Caller finds return value (if any) on eval stack

## 5. P-Code Assembler (pasm) Syntax

### 5.0 File Extensions

- `.spc` — p-code assembler source files (input to pasm)
- `.p24` — assembled p-code bytecode files (output of pasm, input to VM)

### 5.1 Source Format

```
; Comment (semicolon to end of line)

.const UART_DATA  -65280      ; named constant
.const UART_STATUS -65279
.const LED_PORT   -65024

.global counter 1             ; 1 word of global storage
.global buffer 32             ; 32 words

.data msg 72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 0   ; "Hello\0" as bytes

.proc main 0                 ; procedure, 0 locals
    push msg                  ; push address of msg
    call puts
    halt
.end

.proc puts 1                 ; 1 local (loop pointer)
    loada 0                   ; arg0 = string address
    storel 0                  ; local0 = current ptr
loop:
    loadl 0
    loadb                     ; load byte at ptr
    dup
    jz done                   ; if zero, done
    sys 1                     ; PUTC
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                  ; ptr++
    jmp loop
done:
    drop                      ; drop the zero
    ret 1                     ; return, clean 1 arg
.end
```

### 5.2 Directives

| Directive | Syntax | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| `.const` | `.const NAME value` | Define named constant |
| `.global` | `.global NAME nwords` | Reserve global storage |
| `.data` | `.data NAME byte, byte, ...` | Define byte data (string/array) |
| `.proc` | `.proc NAME nlocals` | Begin procedure (nlocals = local slot count) |
| `.end` | `.end` | End procedure |

### 5.3 Labels

Labels appear on their own line, ending with `:`:

```
loop:
    ; instructions...
    jmp loop
```

Labels are local to the enclosing `.proc` / `.end` block.

### 5.4 Integer Literals

- Decimal: `42`, `-1`, `0`
- Hex: `0xFF` (if supported) or decimal equivalents
- Named constants: `UART_DATA` (substituted by assembler)

### 5.5 Instructions

One instruction per line. Operand follows mnemonic separated by whitespace:

```
push 42
loadl 0
jz done
call puts
sys 1
ret 0
```

## 6. Opcode Encoding Table

Opcodes are assigned sequential byte values:

| Byte | Mnemonic | Operand |
|------|----------|---------|
| 0x00 | halt | — |
| 0x01 | push | imm24 |
| 0x02 | push_s | imm8 |
| 0x03 | dup | — |
| 0x04 | drop | — |
| 0x05 | swap | — |
| 0x06 | over | — |
| 0x10 | add | — |
| 0x11 | sub | — |
| 0x12 | mul | — |
| 0x13 | div | — |
| 0x14 | mod | — |
| 0x15 | neg | — |
| 0x16 | and | — |
| 0x17 | or | — |
| 0x18 | xor | — |
| 0x19 | not | — |
| 0x1A | shl | — |
| 0x1B | shr | — |
| 0x20 | eq | — |
| 0x21 | ne | — |
| 0x22 | lt | — |
| 0x23 | le | — |
| 0x24 | gt | — |
| 0x25 | ge | — |
| 0x30 | jmp | addr24 |
| 0x31 | jz | addr24 |
| 0x32 | jnz | addr24 |
| 0x33 | call | addr24 |
| 0x34 | ret | nargs8 |
| 0x35 | calln | depth8 addr24 |
| 0x36 | trap | code8 |
| 0x40 | enter | nlocals8 |
| 0x41 | leave | — |
| 0x42 | loadl | off8 |
| 0x43 | storel | off8 |
| 0x44 | loadg | off24 |
| 0x45 | storeg | off24 |
| 0x46 | addrl | off8 |
| 0x47 | addrg | off24 |
| 0x48 | loada | idx8 |
| 0x49 | storea | idx8 |
| 0x4A | loadn | depth8 off8 |
| 0x4B | storen | depth8 off8 |
| 0x50 | load | — |
| 0x51 | store | — |
| 0x52 | loadb | — |
| 0x53 | storeb | — |
| 0x60 | sys | id8 |

Reserved ranges 0x70–0xFF for future opcodes (heap, debug, extended ops).

## 7. Trap Codes

| Code | Name | Trigger |
|------|------|---------|
| 0 | USER_TRAP | `trap 0` instruction |
| 1 | DIV_ZERO | Division/modulo by zero |
| 2 | STACK_OVERFLOW | Eval or call stack exceeds limit |
| 3 | STACK_UNDERFLOW | Eval stack pop when empty |
| 4 | INVALID_OPCODE | Unknown opcode byte |
| 5 | INVALID_ADDRESS | Memory access out of bounds |
| 6 | NIL_POINTER | Dereference of address 0 |
| 7 | BOUNDS_CHECK | Array index out of bounds |

On trap: VM sets status=2, trap_code=N, halts. Diagnostic message to UART.

## 8. Memory Segment Layout

At VM initialization:

1. **Code segment** starts at a fixed base address (after VM code)
2. **Constants/data** follows code (emitted by pasm)
3. **Globals** follow data (allocated per `.global` directives)
4. **Call stack** starts after globals, grows upward
5. **Eval stack** starts after call stack region, grows upward
6. **Heap** starts after eval stack region, grows upward
7. Segment base addresses stored in VM state struct

Sizes are configurable at VM init time. Default allocations:
- Call stack: 4 KB
- Eval stack: 2 KB
- Heap: remaining SRAM up to ~900 KB

## 9. Implementation Strategy

### Phase 1: Minimal VM
- Opcode dispatch loop
- push, dup, drop, add, sub, halt
- UART output via sys PUTC
- Hardcoded test bytecode (no pasm yet)

### Phase 2: Control Flow + Procedures
- jmp, jz, jnz
- call, ret, enter, leave
- loadl, storel, loada, storea
- Frame management

### Phase 3: Full Instruction Set
- All arithmetic/logic/comparison opcodes
- Globals (loadg, storeg)
- Indirect memory (load, store, loadb, storeb)
- Nonlocal access (loadn, storen)
- All sys calls

### Phase 4: P-Code Assembler
- Lexer for pasm syntax (UART input)
- Two-pass assembly (collect labels, emit bytes)
- Directive handling (.const, .global, .data, .proc/.end)
- Output bytecode to memory buffer

### Phase 5: Integration & Test Suite
- pasm assembles test programs
- VM executes assembled bytecode
- Regression test harness (demo.sh)
- Full test coverage per staged plan

## 10. COR24 Assembly Patterns

Reference patterns from tf24a for common idioms:

### Dispatch Table
```asm
; Index into jump table by opcode byte
; r0 = opcode
la r2, dispatch_table
; multiply r0 by 3 (entry size = .word = 3 bytes)
; ... load target address, jmp
```

### UART Output
```asm
; r0 = character to send
la r2, -65280       ; UART base
tx_wait:
    lb r0, 1(r2)   ; status (sign-extended)
    cls r0, z       ; C = (status < 0) = TX busy
    brt tx_wait
    sb r0, 0(r2)   ; write byte
```

### Memory Struct Access
```asm
; Load vm_state.pc into r0
la fp, vm_state
lw r0, 0(fp)       ; pc at offset 0
```

# Phase 0 Pascal Subset Grammar and .spc Emission Spec

## 1. BNF Grammar

```bnf
program         = "program" IDENT ";" block "." .

block           = [ const-section ] [ var-section ] compound-stmt .

const-section   = "const" const-def { const-def } .
const-def       = IDENT "=" expression ";" .

var-section     = "var" var-decl { var-decl } .
var-decl        = ident-list ":" type-id ";" .
ident-list      = IDENT { "," IDENT } .

type-id         = "integer" | "boolean" .

compound-stmt   = "begin" stmt { ";" stmt } "end" .

stmt            = [ assignment
                  | if-stmt
                  | while-stmt
                  | writeln-stmt
                  | compound-stmt ] .

assignment      = IDENT ":=" expression .

if-stmt         = "if" expression "then" stmt [ "else" stmt ] .

while-stmt      = "while" expression "do" stmt .

writeln-stmt    = "writeln" [ "(" expr-list ")" ] .
expr-list       = expression { "," expression } .

expression      = simple-expr [ rel-op simple-expr ] .
rel-op          = "=" | "<>" | "<" | "<=" | ">" | ">=" .

simple-expr     = [ "+" | "-" ] term { add-op term } .
add-op          = "+" | "-" | "or" .

term            = factor { mul-op factor } .
mul-op          = "*" | "div" | "mod" | "and" .

factor          = IDENT
               | INTEGER
               | "true" | "false"
               | "not" factor
               | "(" expression ")" .
```

### Tokens

| Token     | Pattern                        | Notes                          |
|-----------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| IDENT     | `[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*`      | Case-insensitive               |
| INTEGER   | `[0-9]+`                       | Decimal only, 24-bit range     |
| Keywords  | `program`, `const`, `var`, `begin`, `end`, `if`, `then`, `else`, `while`, `do`, `writeln`, `integer`, `boolean`, `true`, `false`, `div`, `mod`, `and`, `or`, `not` | Reserved, case-insensitive |
| Symbols   | `:=`, `;`, `.`, `,`, `(`, `)`, `+`, `-`, `*`, `=`, `<>`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `:` | |
| Comment   | `{` ... `}` or `(*` ... `*)`   | Skipped by lexer               |

## 2. .spc Code Generation

The compiler emits `.spc` text — p-code assembler source for pasm. All user variables are globals in Phase 0 (no procedures yet, single scope).

### 2.1 Program Structure

```pascal
program Foo;
  { const/var sections }
begin
  { statements }
end.
```

Emits:

```
; Generated by p24p
.global <name> 1          ; one .global per var declaration

.proc main 0              ; program body = main, 0 locals
    enter 0
    ; ... statement code ...
    halt
.end
```

### 2.2 Variable Declarations

Each variable gets a `.global` directive. The compiler tracks global offsets.

```pascal
var x, y: integer;
    flag: boolean;
```

Emits:

```
.global x 1
.global y 1
.global flag 1
```

### 2.3 Constant Declarations

Constants are pure compile-time values. They produce no .spc output — the compiler substitutes the value at every use site.

```pascal
const max = 100;
      ready = true;
```

No .spc emitted. Uses of `max` emit `push 100`. Uses of `ready` emit `push 1`.

### 2.4 Expressions

Expressions compile to stack-based code. Each sub-expression leaves one value on the eval stack.

#### Literals and Identifiers

| Pascal          | .spc                      |
|-----------------|---------------------------|
| `42`            | `push 42`                 |
| `true`          | `push 1`                  |
| `false`         | `push 0`                  |
| `x` (variable)  | `loadg x`                 |
| `max` (constant) | `push 100`               |

#### Arithmetic Operators

| Pascal    | .spc     | Stack Effect       |
|-----------|----------|--------------------|
| `a + b`   | `add`    | ( a b -- a+b )     |
| `a - b`   | `sub`    | ( a b -- a-b )     |
| `a * b`   | `mul`    | ( a b -- a*b )     |
| `a div b` | `div`    | ( a b -- a/b )     |
| `a mod b` | `mod`    | ( a b -- a%b )     |
| `-a`      | `neg`    | ( a -- -a )        |

Binary operators: compile left operand, compile right operand, emit op.

```pascal
x + y * 2
```

Emits:

```
    loadg x
    loadg y

9
8
7
6
5
4
3
2
1

## 1. BNF Grammar

```bnf
program         = "program" IDENT ";" block "." .

block           = [ const-section ] [ var-section ] compound-stmt .

const-section   = "const" const-def { const-def } .
const-def       = IDENT "=" expression ";" .

var-section     = "var" var-decl { var-decl } .
var-decl        = ident-list ":" type-id ";" .
ident-list      = IDENT { "," IDENT } .

type-id         = "integer" | "boolean" .

compound-stmt   = "begin" stmt { ";" stmt } "end" .

stmt            = [ assignment
                  | if-stmt
                  | while-stmt
                  | writeln-stmt
                  | compound-stmt ] .

assignment      = IDENT ":=" expression .

if-stmt         = "if" expression "then" stmt [ "else" stmt ] .

while-stmt      = "while" expression "do" stmt .

writeln-stmt    = "writeln" [ "(" expr-list ")" ] .
expr-list       = expression { "," expression } .

expression      = simple-expr [ rel-op simple-expr ] .
rel-op          = "=" | "<>" | "<" | "<=" | ">" | ">=" .

simple-expr     = [ "+" | "-" ] term { add-op term } .
add-op          = "+" | "-" | "or" .

term            = factor { mul-op factor } .
mul-op          = "*" | "div" | "mod" | "and" .

factor          = IDENT
               | INTEGER
               | "true" | "false"
               | "not" factor
               | "(" expression ")" .
```

|-----------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| IDENT     | `[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*`      | Case-insensitive               |
| INTEGER   | `[0-9]+`                       | Decimal only, 24-bit range     |
| Keywords  | `program`, `const`, `var`, `begin`, `end`, `if`, `then`, `else`, `while`, `do`, `writeln`, `integer`, `boolean`, `true`, `false`, `div`, `mod`, `and`, `or`, `not` | Reserved, case-insensitive |
